name: Managing Shared Code Across Repositories with Subtree
description: Use git subtree to embed and synchronize shared libraries or components across multiple repositories without submodule complexity.
prerequisites: Two or more repositories (a main project and a shared library)

steps.title: Add a remote for the shared library
steps.description: Add the shared library repository as a named remote so you can reference it in subtree commands.

steps.title: Add the subtree to your project
steps.description: Pull the shared library into the lib/shared-ui directory. The --squash flag collapses the library's history into a single merge commit.
steps.tip: Use --squash to keep your project history clean. Without it, the entire library history is merged into your project.
steps.warning: The prefix directory must not already exist. Git will create it and populate it with the library contents.

steps.title: Pull updates from the shared library
steps.description: Fetch and merge the latest changes from the shared library into your project's subtree directory.

steps.title: Make local changes to the subtree
steps.command:
# Edit files in lib/shared-ui/ as normal
git add lib/shared-ui/
git commit -m "fix: correct button hover state in shared-ui"
steps.description: You can modify subtree files directly in your project and commit them normally. These changes live in your project's history.

steps.title: Push local changes back to the shared library
steps.description: Extract commits that touched the subtree directory and push them to the shared library repository on a new branch.
steps.tip: Push to a feature branch on the shared library and create a pull request, rather than pushing directly to main.

steps.title: Split subtree into a standalone branch
steps.description: Extract the subtree history into a standalone branch. This is useful for initially creating a shared library from code that started in a monorepo.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# git-subtree-manage.sh — Subtree operations helper
set -euo pipefail

ACTION="${1:?Usage: $0 {add|pull|push|split} <prefix> <remote> [branch]}"
PREFIX="${2:?Prefix directory required}"
REMOTE="${3:-}"
BRANCH="${4:-main}"

case "$ACTION" in
  add)
    if [ -z "$REMOTE" ]; then
      echo "Usage: $0 add <prefix> <remote-url> [branch]"
      exit 1
    fi
    REMOTE_NAME=$(basename "$REMOTE" .git)
    echo "Adding remote '$REMOTE_NAME'..."
    git remote add "$REMOTE_NAME" "$REMOTE" 2>/dev/null || echo "Remote already exists."
    git fetch "$REMOTE_NAME"
    echo "Adding subtree at $PREFIX from $REMOTE_NAME/$BRANCH..."
    git subtree add --prefix="$PREFIX" "$REMOTE_NAME" "$BRANCH" --squash
    echo "Done. Shared code is at $PREFIX/"
    ;;
  pull)
    REMOTE_NAME="${REMOTE:?Remote name required}"
    echo "Pulling updates into $PREFIX from $REMOTE_NAME/$BRANCH..."
    git subtree pull --prefix="$PREFIX" "$REMOTE_NAME" "$BRANCH" --squash
    echo "Updated."
    ;;
  push)
    REMOTE_NAME="${REMOTE:?Remote name required}"
    echo "Pushing subtree changes from $PREFIX to $REMOTE_NAME/$BRANCH..."
    git subtree push --prefix="$PREFIX" "$REMOTE_NAME" "$BRANCH"
    echo "Pushed."
    ;;
  split)
    SPLIT_BRANCH="${REMOTE:-${PREFIX//\//-}-standalone}"
    echo "Splitting $PREFIX into branch '$SPLIT_BRANCH'..."
    git subtree split --prefix="$PREFIX" -b "$SPLIT_BRANCH"
    echo "Branch '$SPLIT_BRANCH' created with subtree history."
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Usage: $0 {add|pull|push|split} <prefix> <remote> [branch]"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

faq.question: What is the difference between git subtree and git submodule?
faq.answer: Subtree embeds the library code directly in your repository, so cloning is simple and the code is always present. Submodule creates a pointer to another repository that must be separately initialized and updated. Subtree is simpler for consumers but can make history more complex.

faq.question: Does the --squash flag affect pushing changes back?
faq.answer: Yes. When you use --squash during add and pull, you must consistently use --squash for all operations. Mixing squashed and non-squashed operations on the same subtree can cause merge conflicts and history issues.

faq.question: Can I use subtree with repositories I do not own?
faq.answer: Yes, you can pull from any public repository. However, you can only push back if you have write access. For third-party libraries, subtree works well as a vendoring mechanism where you pull updates but do not push back.
